part: A German Farm Family in Metropolis
title: Born in Paducah, Raised in Metropolis
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Louisville in August 2013, Kay Tillow recounts being born in Paducah, Kentucky in 1942 and raised across the river in Metropolis, Illinois, a town of six thousand, in a family of modest means descended from German immigrants who each held eighty-acre farms, her mother a teacher trained at Southern Illinois and her father a furniture-store owner.

title: The Wall Between
summary: Tillow describes a Presbyterian minister, Maynard Elftman, giving her Anne Braden's book The Wall Between in high school, an eye-opening revelation from a family that talked morals and education but never politics, and how the book told of the Bradens buying a Louisville home for a Black family, the bombing that followed, and the sedition charge of trying to overthrow Kentucky.

title: We Weren't Ready for That
summary: She recalls the integration of the grade schools in Metropolis, the upset it caused, Black teachers losing their jobs, and a teacher telling her, when she nominated a young African American girl to lead a high-school club, that they were not ready for that, all before she graduated in 1960.

title: Discrimination Is Stinkin'
summary: At the University of Illinois Tillow joined the NAACP and walked her first picket line against a store that would not hire African Americans, frightened but certain it was right, carrying a sign reading that in the land of Lincoln discrimination is stinkin', and traveled with the local NAACP to SNCC demonstrations and a segregated courtroom in Cairo, Illinois, where she saw Mary McCollum, gashed on the thigh during a demonstration.

title: You Don't Know Du Bois?
summary: Tillow recounts her 1962 junior year abroad in Ghana, where W.E.B. Du Bois received an honorary degree to work on his Encyclopedia Africana and where students were astonished she had never heard of him, exposing the holes in American education, and where she learned from chancellor Conor Cruise O'Brien; she returned in 1963 to find Du Bois had died in August and the March on Washington had just happened, and later met John Lewis at Illinois.

title: I Couldn't Stay in School
summary: She describes her NAACP chapter sending a delegation to Atlanta over the semester break, where a discussion with Prathia Hall of Philadelphia and James Forman led her to decide she had to leave school and help, and then going to Hattiesburg, Mississippi, an oppressive and violent place where Bob Moses had been jailed and a white movement worker so badly beaten by white prisoners he could hardly be recognized as human, where police marched in military ranks to the courthouse and a mass meeting of hundreds gave each other courage in the area where Schwerner, Goodman, and Chaney would soon be killed.

title: Cairo Could Have Vied With Hattiesburg
summary: Asked whether she identified as a Southerner, Tillow says she claimed Kentucky as her birthplace, notes that southern Illinois towns like Cairo could rival Hattiesburg for the brutality of their response to the movement, and explains that after a school break visiting these places she resolved to go back, young and without particular skills but determined to be there and learn.

title: Mentors to the Young People
summary: Tillow describes working with the Appalachian Committee for Full Employment among coal miners and meeting Walter at Highlander Folk School, and how Carl and Anne Braden, along with James Dombrowski, mentored the young people coming through the South, sharing their newspaper The Southern Patriot, having them read works like Eleanor Flexner's Century of Struggle, and, out of Carl's conviction about the corrosive impact of anti-communism drawn from his own sedition case, shaping SNCC's refusal to adopt the liberal anti-communism of Hubert Humphrey.

title: March on Frankfort for Jobs
summary: Tillow explains being drawn to the argument that public accommodations alone could not end inequality because it had an economic base, describes a group of coal miners arrested for conspiracy while trying to revive their broken union, her newspaper Kentucky Jobs with Justice, and the March on Frankfort for Jobs and Freedom on March 5, 1964, where Appalachian coalfield contingents joined Louisville organizers.

title: From the Movement Into the Unions
summary: She recalls a Highlander workshop on unions during the SNCC era, drawing on Highlander's earlier role educating CIO organizers trying to bring Black and white workers together in the South, with a United Electrical Workers speaker leading a discussion of what role unions should play in justice and equality, and describes how, as people left SNCC by 1965 and 1966 with Walter having worked on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in 1964, she married Walter around 1964, the two went to the electrical workers union and were sent to separate cities, and she spent essentially all of 1964 organizing in East Kentucky.

title: A Black Union in White Hospitals
summary: She recounts leaving the United Electrical Workers for hospital workers union 1199 in 1969 and establishing it in Pennsylvania where the workforce was not majority Black, winning the first hospital in Lewistown despite an employer campaign branding it a Black union, organizing registered nurses at Mercy Hospital in Wilkes-Barre, and notes the union's reach into Baltimore and Johns Hopkins, where Coretta Scott King helped, and the hard-fought Charleston, South Carolina campaign that did not win recognition.

title: The Grave of Myles Horton
summary: Tillow describes Highlander's locations over the years, burned down and relocated to a big house in Knoxville, and a later return with a single-payer colleague from Tennessee to visit the grave of Myles Horton, with a play about Horton and Don West and a historical marker she photographed with Walter.

title: No Law Covered Hospital Workers
summary: She explains that when 1199 began in Pittsburgh in 1969 other unions saw it as a New York intruder on their territory while the United Electrical Workers, David Montgomery, and Tom Quinn formed a support committee, that the National Labor Relations Act did not yet cover hospital workers so recognition had to be forced by movement pressure, and that a 1970 strike at Presbyterian Hospital, though it won no bargaining, led Governor Milton Shapp to add hospital workers to state collective-bargaining coverage.

title: A New Kind of Challenge
summary: Tillow notes that Pennsylvania became a state that legally allowed hospital workers to organize and that the National Labor Relations Board did not amend its coverage nationally until about 1975, and describes the Pittsburgh service workforce as only thirty or forty percent African American, unlike New York, making it a new challenge they were proud to meet.

title: A Sit-In on Christmas Eve
summary: She recounts spreading the union to Uniontown, where families with coal-mining traditions wanted it and a strike was waged but not won, the tactic of shutting down the enterprises of hospital board members, and the first union victory at the Jewish Home and Hospital for the Aged in Pittsburgh, a majority African American campaign led by Henry Nicholas, who later headed the Philadelphia hospital workers, settled after a solid Christmas Eve 1969 sit-in.

title: A Rabbi to Hold the Election
summary: Tillow describes the sit-in holding firm, telling management they would send help only for emergencies, dismissing the laundry worker's demands, and winning by day's end an agreement for a secret-ballot election overseen by a rabbi from Rodef Shalom, which when won established the first 1199 union in Pennsylvania, soon followed under the new state law by Lewistown, Wilkes-Barre, and Washington, many of them majority-white bargaining units that proved people could unite on economic issues with civil rights understanding.

title: My Mother Crying on the Phone
summary: Tillow recounts her family's unhappiness that she left school before graduating, not finishing college until 1967, their fear that the work was dangerous, and the FBI visiting her parents over her anti-war activity, her mother crying on the phone believing she was in terrible trouble, a repression meant to make participation in the movement difficult.

title: We Didn't Come All This Way for Two Seats
summary: She describes seeing all her work as connected, the civil rights movement as the foundation and catalyst that made other organizing possible, and draws strength from common people of humble origins who shook the nation, recalling seeing Fannie Lou Hamer many times and her line that they did not come all this way for two seats, and Diane Nash telling the Justice Department that the freedom riders had all made out their wills.

part: Union Women and a New Fight
title: It Wasn't the Right Time on the Agenda
summary: Tillow describes her involvement in pay equity and serving as president of the Pittsburgh Coalition of Labor Union Women, repeatedly denied recognition at the Allegheny Central Labor Council, and the founding of CLUW in 1974, which gave union women an organization to speak on women's, civil rights, and anti-war issues, with a dynamic Pittsburgh chapter often told it was out of line, even as she notes the labor movement's deep problems and losing ground on wages.

title: Make Health Care a Right
summary: Tillow describes her current work building the nurses union in Louisville and the single-payer movement for universal health care, championing Congressman John Conyers's bill H.R. 676, which would remove the private for-profit insurance industry and for-profit hospitals so no one could profit from denying care, and her hope that health care, touching everyone, could be the place to break through and think big rather than settle for tiny incremental change.

title: Overworked to Incompetence
summary: She argues that health care, education, and the military should not be run as businesses, voices concern about charter privatization undermining public schools and praises the Chicago teachers who took it on, and recounts learning from nurses in Louisville that the profit motive at Humana-owned hospitals understaffed nursing units to the point that even an excellent nurse could be overworked to incompetence and patient care suffered.

title: Six Hundred Five Unions
summary: Tillow recounts that H.R. 676 was first introduced in 2003 and that she founded the All Unions Committee for Single Payer Health Care, winning her first endorsements from her CLUW chapter and the Chicago CLUW, then the national CLUW and the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, building to 605 unions, 43 state AFL-CIO federations, 146 central labor councils, and 22 international unions, while arguing against the insurance-company thinking that treats people using care as a loss.

title: Medicare Desegregated the Hospitals
summary: She connects universal health care to closing the disparity between African Americans and whites, recalling that when Medicare passed in 1965 the law denied money to segregated hospitals, forcing Southern hospitals to integrate, and citing a study associated with David Satcher and Adewale Troutman estimating tens of thousands of excess deaths in the African American community that would not have occurred under equality, much of it tied to lack of access to care.

title: Go Forward or Go Backward
summary: Tillow speaks of having to go forward or fall backward, citing the threat to voting rights after Shelby County v. Holder, and names her husband Walter as her source of strength, amazing in his persistence and shaped by a different left perspective from her farm background, reflecting that many couples met in the South during a period when a movement lifted everyone, and that she gained more from the movement than she gave.

title: The Movement Had No Pension Plan
summary: She observes that the movement offered no pension plan and that many veterans struggle, advocates expanding Social Security by taxing all wages and capital gains rather than capping the tax, and describes a resolution to the upcoming AFL-CIO convention, backed by eight central labor councils, to protect and expand Social Security and Medicare, fold everyone into Medicare as the single-payer plan, and call a March on Washington.

title: Used to Divide People
summary: Tillow warns that immigrants are being used to divide people much as African Americans once were, notes that the Conyers bill excludes no one while the Affordable Care Act bars the undocumented from even buying insurance though their payroll contributions to Medicare and Social Security go uncollected, and calls the rising number of African Americans in prison deeply troubling, welcoming the judge's ruling against stop and frisk.

title: What a Movement Is Made Of
summary: Closing the interview, Tillow appreciates the chance to talk about matters she had not considered in a long time, defers to others who played more prominent roles, and affirms what she says the project has shown strongly, that a movement is made of all the people doing their pieces and how they all connect.
